annotations.label: Formal language

anchor.snippet: a formal language is a set of strings whose symbols are taken from a set called

annotations.note: `Language α := Set (List α)` directly formalizes a formal language as a set of strings over an alphabet `α`.

annotations.label: Alphabet (formal languages)

anchor.snippet: An alphabet , in the context of formal languages, can be any set

annotations.note: Mathlib has no standalone `Alphabet` definition; the alphabet is the implicit type parameter `α` of `Language α`.

annotations.label: Word over an alphabet

anchor.snippet: A word over an alphabet can be any finite sequence

annotations.note: Words are not given their own Mathlib name; they are represented as `List α` (per the file docstring "strings are implemented as lists over the alphabet").

annotations.label: Kleene star notation for all words

anchor.snippet: The set of all words over an alphabet

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a distinguished notation for the set of all words over an alphabet (it would just be `Set.univ : Set (List α)` or the top element of `Language α`).

annotations.label: Length of a word

anchor.snippet: The length of a word is the number of letters it is composed of

annotations.note: Word length is `List.length`, which lives in Lean core (`Init`), not in `Mathlib/`.

annotations.label: Empty word

anchor.snippet: there is only one word of length 0, the empty word

annotations.note: The empty word is `[] : List α` (`List.nil`), which is defined in Lean core, not in `Mathlib/`.

annotations.label: Concatenation of words

anchor.snippet: By concatenation one can combine two words to form a new word

annotations.note: Word concatenation is `List.append` / `++`, defined in Lean core, not in `Mathlib/`.

annotations.label: Formal language over an alphabet

anchor.snippet: a formal language

annotations.note: `Language α := Set (List α)` is exactly a subset of the strings over alphabet `α`.

annotations.label: Well-formed word

anchor.snippet: a word

annotations.note: Well-formedness is encoded as membership in the language via `Membership (List α) (Language α)`; no separate `WellFormed` predicate exists.

annotations.label: Arithmetic-string language L

anchor.snippet: The following rules describe a formal language L over the alphabet

annotations.note: This particular toy example language is not constructed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Membership in L: "23+4=555"

anchor.snippet: Under these rules, the string

annotations.note: The specific membership claim for the toy example is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite language enumeration

anchor.snippet: For finite languages, one can explicitly enumerate all well-formed words

annotations.note: No dedicated `FiniteLanguage` construction or enumeration lemma is provided; finite languages are just `Set.Finite` languages.

annotations.label: Empty language

anchor.snippet: the empty language , which contains no words at all

annotations.note: `(0 : Language α) := (∅ : Set _)` (also documented as `Language.zero_def`) is exactly the empty language.

annotations.label: Infinite formal languages

anchor.snippet: Here are some examples of formal languages

annotations.note: The specific examples listed in the article (e.g. balanced parentheses, palindromes) are not constructed by name in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Concatenation of languages

anchor.snippet: The concatenation

annotations.note: `Mul (Language α)` is defined as `image2 (· ++ ·)`, exactly the language concatenation, with characterization `Language.mem_mul`.

annotations.label: Intersection of languages

anchor.snippet: consists of all strings that are contained in both languages

annotations.note: Intersection comes from the inherited `CompleteAtomicBooleanAlgebra` on `Language α`, with `mem_inf : x ∈ l ⊓ m ↔ x ∈ l ∧ x ∈ m`.

annotations.label: Complement of a language

anchor.snippet: consists of all strings over

annotations.note: Complement `lᶜ` is inherited from the `BooleanAlgebra` instance on `Language α` (see `Language.compl_compl`).

annotations.label: Kleene star of a language

anchor.snippet: the language consisting of all words that are concatenations of zero or more words in the original language

annotations.note: `KStar (Language α)` and `Language.kstar_def` define `l∗` as the set of flattenings of lists of words drawn from `l`.

annotations.label: Reversal of a language

anchor.snippet: Reversal

annotations.note: `Language.reverse l := { w | w.reverse ∈ l }` exactly formalizes the reversal of a language.

annotations.label: Closure under an operation

anchor.snippet: A class of languages is closed under a particular operation

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a general "class of languages is closed under an operation" predicate; individual closure results are stated ad hoc.

annotations.label: Closure properties of context-free languages

anchor.snippet: the context-free languages are known to be closed under union, concatenation, and intersection with regular languages

annotations.note: Mathlib proves only closure of context-free languages under reversal (`Language.IsContextFree.reverse`); closure under union, concatenation, and intersection with regular languages is not present.

annotations.label: Formal theory

anchor.snippet: a formal theory is a set of sentences expressed in a formal language

annotations.note: `FirstOrder.Language.Theory := Set L.Sentence` is exactly a set of sentences in a (first-order) formal language.

annotations.label: Formal system

anchor.snippet: A formal system

annotations.note: Mathlib has no standalone notion of "formal system" (alphabet + grammar + inference rules) as a single object.

annotations.label: Formal proof / derivation

anchor.snippet: A formal proof or derivation is a finite sequence of well-formed formulas

annotations.note: No syntactic deduction/proof-tree object exists in Mathlib's ModelTheory; consequence is only defined semantically via `Theory.Model` / `⊨`.

annotations.label: Theorem of a formal system

anchor.snippet: The last sentence in the sequence is a theorem of a formal system

annotations.note: Without a syntactic deduction relation, Mathlib has no "theorem of a formal system" predicate; only semantic entailment via `Theory.ModelsBoundedFormula` is available.

annotations.label: Interpretation of a formal language

anchor.snippet: an interpretation assigns a meaning to each of the formulas

annotations.note: `FirstOrder.Language.Structure` interprets function and relation symbols on a carrier type, providing the standard model-theoretic interpretation.

annotations.label: Model of a formula

anchor.snippet: a model for a formula is an interpretation of terms such that the formula becomes true

annotations.note: `Theory.Model T M` (notation `M ⊨ T`), together with `Sentence.Realize`, formalizes a model of a (set of) formulas in a first-order language.
